1: shadow O LORD, God of vengeance, O God of vengeance, shine forth! God of vengeance shining forth — the parousia of Christ: when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God (2 Thessalonians 1:7-8). The shining forth is the glory of Christ's return; the vengeance is the just judgment committed to the Son (John 5:22).
2: shadow Rise up, O judge of the earth; repay to the proud what they deserve! The Judge of the earth rising — Christ the appointed judge: he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead (Acts 17:31). The resurrection is the credential of the Judge; the proud are repaid at the last day.
3: shadow O LORD, how long shall the wicked, how long shall the wicked exult? The how long of the martyrs — how long before you will judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth? (Revelation 6:10). Christ's answer is the promise of vindication at the set time. The wicked exulting at the cross seemed victorious; the resurrection was the first answer to how long; the parousia is the final one.
4: theme They pour out their arrogant words; all the evildoers boast. Arrogant words against God — the speech of those who mocked Christ on the cross (Matthew 27:39-44) and those who blaspheme throughout history. Whoever speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven (Matthew 12:32). The boasting of evildoers faces the silencing judgment of the one who speaks with divine authority.
5: shadow They crush your people, O LORD, and afflict your heritage. The crushing of God's people — the suffering of believers patterned on Christ's own suffering: a servant is not greater than his master. If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you (John 15:20). Christ identifies with the crushed: as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me (Matthew 25:40).
6: shadow They kill the widow and the sojourner, and murder the fatherless; The murder of the vulnerable — the oppression Christ came to address (Luke 4:18 — good news to the poor; release to the captives). His identification with the least (Matthew 25:40) means every murdered widow and orphan is an offense against Christ himself. The Judge who rises to defend them is the one who cared for them in his earthly ministry.
7: shadow and they say, The LORD does not see; the God of Jacob does not perceive. God does not see — the arrogance of those who sinned in the dark, thinking God absent. Christ exposes this: nothing is hidden that will not be revealed, or secret that will not be known (Matthew 10:26). Before him all things are naked and exposed (Hebrews 4:13); his eyes like flames of fire see everything (Revelation 1:14).
8: theme Understand, O dullest of the people! Fools, when will you be wise? The call to wisdom — Christ calls the foolish to wisdom: come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... learn from me (Matthew 11:28-29). The wisdom that the fool lacks is the wisdom from above (James 3:17); it is found in Christ who became to us wisdom from God (1 Corinthians 1:30).
9: shadow He who planted the ear, does he not hear? He who formed the eye, does he not see? The Creator who hears and sees — Christ demonstrates this in John 9: he sees the blind man (John 9:1), heals his eyes (John 9:6-7), and then announces: For judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind (John 9:39). The one who formed the eye comes to restore sight.
10: shadow He who disciplines nations, does he not rebuke? He who teaches man knowledge — God who disciplines nations and teaches knowledge — Christ rebukes and teaches with divine authority (Matthew 7:29 — he taught them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes). His rebukes to the Pharisees (Matt 23) and his teaching of the nations (Matthew 28:20 — teaching them to observe all that I have commanded) fulfill this verse.
11: direct the LORD — knows the thoughts of man, that they are but a breath. 1 Corinthians 3:20 quotes this verse: The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, that they are futile. Paul uses it to demolish human wisdom-boasting and ground all confidence in Christ crucified. The God who knows human thoughts as futile — including the thoughts of those who crucified his Son — is the one who raises the foolish things of the world to shame the wise.
12: shadow Blessed is the man whom you discipline, O LORD, and whom you teach out of your law, The discipline of the LORD — applied to believers in Hebrews 12:5-11 (citing Proverbs 3:11-12): the Lord disciplines the one he loves. Christ himself learned obedience through what he suffered (Hebrews 5:8) — the ultimate example of divine discipline producing the fruit of righteousness. His disciples are discipled (the same root) by following him through suffering.
13: shadow to give him rest from days of trouble, until a pit is dug for the wicked. Rest from trouble — the rest Christ provides: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest (Matthew 11:28). The ultimate rest is the sabbath rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9-11), entered through faith in Christ. The pit dug for the wicked is the eschatological judgment of those who rejected him.
14: shadow For the LORD will not forsake his people; he will not abandon his heritage. The LORD will not forsake — Christ's great promise: I am with you always, to the end of the age (Matthew 28:20). Paul's certainty: I am sure that neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord (Romans 8:38-39). The never-forsaking God is the Christ who went through abandonment on the cross so he might be the unforsaking Shepherd forever.
15: shadow for justice will return to the righteous, and all the upright in heart will follow it. Justice returning to the righteous — the eschatological reversal. Christ is the righteous one (Acts 3:14 — you denied the Holy and Righteous One) to whom justice returns at the resurrection. All who are upright in heart — those clothed in his righteousness (Philippians 3:9) — follow in his vindication.
16: shadow Who rises up for me against the wicked? Who stands up for me against evildoers? Who rises up? — Christ rises up for the oppressed. Acts 7:55-56: Stephen sees Jesus standing at the right hand of God — he rises to welcome his martyr. The one who rose from the dead rises for those who are persecuted. He stands as advocate (1 John 2:1 — we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous).
17: shadow If the LORD had not been my help, my soul would soon have lived in the land of silence. The LORD as help — Hebrews 13:6 applies a similar promise directly: I will never leave you nor forsake you, leading to the declaration The Lord is my helper; I will not fear. The land of silence is death — from which Christ rescues those who trust him. He himself passed through the land of silence (the tomb) and came out, ensuring none of his own stay there.
18: shadow When I thought, My foot slips, your steadfast love, O LORD, held me up. Steadfast love holding up the slipping foot — the preserving grace of Christ. Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy (Jude 24). The Shepherd who prays for Peter (Luke 22:32 — I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail) holds up the slipping foot.
19: shadow When the cares of my heart are many, your consolations cheer my soul. Divine consolation — the Spirit as Comforter (John 14:16-17 — I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper). The consolations that cheer the soul are the consolations of Christ given through the Spirit: if there is any comfort in Christ... any participation in the Spirit... complete my joy (Philippians 2:1-2).
20: shadow Can wicked rulers be allied with you, who frame injustice by statute? Unjust rulers framing injustice by statute — the Roman and Jewish authorities who condemned Christ through legal process while he was innocent. Pilate found no fault (Luke 23:4), yet sentenced him; the Sanhedrin condemned him on charges of blasphemy against their own manipulated law. Injustice by statute is the mechanism of the Passion.
21: shadow They band together against the life of the righteous and condemn the innocent to death. Banding against the righteous to condemn the innocent — Acts 4:27: for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel. The banding together against Christ is the supreme fulfillment of this verse.
22: shadow But the LORD has become my stronghold, and my God the rock of my refuge. The LORD as stronghold and rock — Christ the rock of refuge (1 Corinthians 10:4). The vindicated righteous one finds refuge in the God who raised him; those condemned by the same injustice find in Christ their stronghold. The rock that was struck to bring forth water (Exod 17) is the same rock that is the refuge from every storm.
23: shadow He will bring back on them their iniquity and wipe them out for their evil; the LORD our God will wipe them out. Iniquity brought back — the principle of divine justice at the last judgment. Christ will judge: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done (Revelation 22:12). The wiping out of evildoers is the work of the one who first wiped out the sin of those who trust him (Colossians 2:14 — canceling the record of debt).
